topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a cold, heavy stone. My stomach clenched immediately, a tight, nauseating knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes: "Regarding your recent piece, 'The Silent Tide,' published under the name of Alistair Finch." My breath hitched. Alistair Finch. That was my name. The meticulous research, the late nights wrestling with the narrative—it had all been borrowed. A wave of profound deflation washed over me, leaving me feeling strangely hollowed out. I reread the attribution, the sheer audacity of the situation making my hands tremble. I slowly closed the laptop, the click of the lid sounding deafening in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, the chill of the glass seeping into my skin.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly. The headline mocked me: "Groundbreaking Analysis by Alistair Finch." A slow, burning heat rose in my chest, a painful pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. I traced the author's name with a finger, the letters feeling alien and wrong. It was a sickening realization, the feeling of having watched someone else build a structure and then finding out the foundation was mine. A low sound escaped my throat, a choked whisper of disbelief. I scrubbed a hand over my face, trying to physically wipe away the sudden sting of humiliation. Every muscle in my jaw felt tight, rigid with a helpless kind of despair. I wanted to disappear, to dissolve into the floorboards, anything to escape the spotlight this revelation had suddenly thrown onto me.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I didn't move for a full minute. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, paralyzed by a strange, heavy inertia. Then, the realization crashed in—my work, my voice, packaged and sold under a different identity. A tremor ran through my hands, making the mouse slip slightly. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under the sudden shift in my posture. My gaze dropped to my hands, noticing the faint tremor, the way my knuckles were white. A deep, weary sigh escaped me, ragged and thin. It felt like a physical weight had been lifted, replaced by something far heavier: the crushing burden of having been duped. I just sat there, letting the silence stretch, the air thick with unspoken regret.
